item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should this have tags for the Daevites and CotBG? Or does that depend on whether it's an artifact that belongs to one of those groups vs. an artifact that just reflects their existence?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The exposition is well written, and doesn't actually "explain everything," we still don't know what causes people to remember, and it's implied that there are more events that have been purged from public knowledge. This gets a +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: a clockwork universe… and the blacked out text (the one that is supposedly what the europa statue is an allegory for) has enough letters to spell MEKHANE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know for a fact that if I saw Napoleon Crossing the Urals while on vacation in real life, I would be lecturing my family members about the history behind the painting the moment I set my eyes on it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, conversely, the strongest empires in history have invariably succumbed, not merely to enemy states, but their own inevitable decay - but the Daeva are no longer a mere empire in history. Conventional warfare no longer matters for them. A time-active culture fundamentally cannot lose a war to a non-time-active culture.

Actually, come to think of it… Ritual death cults who rewrite history to preserve their existence? In some crossover universe, the Daeva could easily be a splinter group of Faction Paradox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda wish we were given another, completely contradictory interpretation here- so that the whole Daeva/Mekhane thing would just be what that particular guy thinks of the painting, and not necessarily even kind of close to the truth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, this SCP just hits all of the right notes for me. I love history, I love the Daevas, and I love the subtle details the author reveals and the ones they wisely choose to redact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, the other expunged word is Mekhane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That had never even occurred to me. They're basically sucking later and later in history, but they still suck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Always happy to see more Daeva shenanigans afoot!

By the way, the other expunged word has to be "Mekhane", right?

I love how the Daeva, unpleasant as they sound, are terrible at actual warfare and at making any kind of strategic ties, so they have to fall back on these anomalous devices to even have a history, let alone a future. They're scary, but they really kind of suck at anything remotely practical. Which is wonderful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is what y'all were up to while I was gone? Damn, I missed out on the fun.

This is really cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mine too :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(and I feel like the author could have emphasized this with another interview of someone who has no historical knowledge saying "It is a painting of some old fashioned soldiers or something")

Or someone with only a casual knowledge, who recognized it as "that famous painting of Napoleon defeating that scary empire", but unable to provide anything more specific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this before I figured out the Daevite connection, just for the "shard from a parallel reality" aspect, which I thought was well-done.

Once I saw that this was the Daevites creeping forward in history (if not our own history, then one too close to it for comfort), I actually shuddered. Enthusiastic +1.

Edited to add: part of what made it work so well is the the historical believably of the painting style and symbology. It really felt like it could be from an only-slightly-different version of the time-period. I can see the painting in my head in the style of other paintings from the time.

I'm also really digging the retcon headcannon @TophatLord's posted above.

Edited again: I also like how the art historian simply naming the painting raises so many questions. First… clearly a different timeline, since even with my limited knowledge of history, I'm pretty sure the closest our timeline's Napoleon came to "Crossing the Urals" was his disastrous (for him) march on Moscow, where the winter weather killed off most of his men. Second… why? Why would someone from Western Europe lead an army across the Urals to attack Siberia? And then have this commemorated as one of the greatest moments in his career?

I also like the subtle touches like the art historian referring to Napoleon's Continental (not French) Army… it implies that in this timeline, Europe has joined forces against a greater common threat, without coming out and saying so.

Edited yet again: Perhaps all the fighting between European countries during that period in our own timeline is a ripple-effect from the great war against the Daevites?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose those points do make sense, and calling it by its designation doesn't seem particularly natural. I've changed "SCP-2515-A" to "the painting" in the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is another question of whether calling an object strictly by its designation is a mark of professionalism. Not to attack this article in particular, it seems more like a trend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a question of why give D-Class access to any classified information that isn't strictly needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Context clues. What else would that question be referring to in this context? Also, before the interview, he could have been told that he was going to be looking at SCP-2515-A, but not what SCP-2515-A actually is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
who was the artist behind SCP-2515-A

How does D-77-9275 know the painting is identified as SCP-2515-A? Judging by their earlier dialogue, he wasn't supposed to know anything about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found the description to be pretty basic, a painting that alters your memory, but the addenda hooked me, hard. This is an article very much appealing to my specific niche taste, and while I agree with other commenters that some additional content might be due, I love what you have here already. +1, this might be one of my favorite Series III articles. Definitely in my top ten.

Also

Site-77

ayyyyyyyyyyy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had a chance to look over your edit.

For me, it improved the article greatly, enough to push me into the up vote direction.

Here's your +1.

Don't spend it all in one place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that, given the amount of confusion, my attempts to hint at the identity of the blackboxed deity have probably been too subtle. I've more or less completely rewritten that part of the interview, to the point where I think it should be much easier to distinguish what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the effect of imparting knowledge is nothing that I would call particularly ground breaking.

People keep saying this, but think that is a very misleading description of what the effect is (it would be like saying that the effect of SCP-2140 is that only authorized personel can read it!). It doesn't, notably, impart any information inconsistent with the viewers background (and I feel like the author could have emphasized this with another interview of someone who has no historical knowledge saying "It is a painting of some old fashioned soldiers or something"). The effect here, by my reading, is that in proximity to the painting people are who they would be without the 1822 reality quake that restored "normal history". The painting is the center of a bubble of that unaltered history. Which in my mind is much more interesting than merely "imparting anomalous knowledge".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an article that gave me mixed feelings. The containment procedures did their job and really drew me into the piece, as I generally like anomalous art. However, while the description of the painting is quite interesting, the effect of imparting knowledge is nothing that I would call particularly ground breaking. As a result, the contents of the article really did hinge on the contents of the interview.

I found the interview to be somewhat enjoyable, it really builds up from being a pretty standard art historian description of the piece to a full blown demonstration of the anomalous information that the article imparts. However, there was a lot blacked out here, so much so that I really didn’t grasp the intention until I delved into the comments, which I find to be poor form.

I realize that in writing an SCP article there needs to be bit of mystery. That’s what gets the gears turning in the reader’s head and really gets the comments flowing. However, if you redact or black out too much, you leave people in the dark, much like those poor souls in that fortress you described. For me as a reader, showing what was under the black boxes probably would have been sufficient, but hey, that’s just this reader’s preference.

I’m going to no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that works way better. Not enough enough to change my vote, but it does make the interview more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this on, but I'm not sure I get whats going on

Headcanon
Retcon Headcanon

So, I'm under the impression that this stems from a former past timeline in which napoleon crushed the Daeva, under the diving guidance of some deity, possibly the Broken God, as stated above. However, this is where the headcanon starts, the daevites were disastrously defeated their civilization couldn't go on (their chronicle had written itself into a corner, or SCP-140-A or his ancestor was killed (I'm unsure how log Daeva live)). So rather than changing the future, they changed the past. They destroyed the timeline in which Napoleon defeated them and go back to when they were last destroyed (the Mongols). Without the Daeva as a threat, whoever is helping Napoleon doesn't show, and we get our present timeline. However, this timeline keeps happening, so the Daevites introduce other means of manipulation and hope that eventually they get something different. Furthermore, as a result of all this timeline manipulation, artifacts star bubbling into the present from alternate timelines.

edit:BTW +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that's much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Furthermore, once their enemies were close at hand, they could perform their Rite of Consumption, and fling jelly-beans at them.
Yeah, could have probably done something more interesting with that sentence rather than plug an expungement.

I wanted to leave the details of the Rite of Consumption somewhat vague, but I agree expunging all description of it was not the most effective way to do so. I've modified that particular part of the interview to something a little bit more descriptive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There isn't enough in this one for me. Maybe it's because I'm not that much into historical art, thus can't really appreciate the details of the explanation, but I think it's mostly because the effect is rather dull. I can't say this is short enough for an anomalous item, mostly because of the length of the exposition, but it doesn't feel worthy of SCP classification. Reading the comments, it seems that one of the hooks is this being about the Daeva, which I'm gonna honestly say I don't remember much about, and I don't much like the idea of having to read another article to get the main meat of this one. Interconnected articles are cool, articles in which another article must be read first don't sit well with me.

Furthermore, once their enemies were close at hand, they could perform their Rite of Consumption, and [DATA EXPUNGED].

Furthermore, once their enemies were close at hand, they could perform their Rite of Consumption, and fling jelly-beans at them.
Yeah, could have probably done something more interesting with that sentence rather than plug an expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since interpreting what the deity in SCP-2515 is seems to be the main point of confusion, I've added a bit of dialogue to the interview that might point you in the right direction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll send you a PM with my interpretation of what the blackboxing with regards to the blackboxed deity is supposed to mean. Don't want to spoil it too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not in my headcanon :P

Edit: And even though that makes more sense based on 140, it's still nice to see an article with a timeline of their defeat played out, especially one that is clearly delineated from the primary Foundation timeline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't losing in the 19th century versus losing in the 13th century, actually mean they are winning, strategically?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's difficult to express how much I love this, if only because it's cathartic to see the Daevites losing, and losing to Napoleon to boot. An entire history of the Daeva getting their teeth kicked in unfolds on its own in my mind. +1

The Daeva connection clinched it for me,1 but I'll admit the business with the blackboxes was pretty tedious. A lot of them are the same length as both Europa and Christ (and Mekane, as sirpudding points out), so trying to plug in deity names to make sense of sentences became a bit more of a chore than a fun exploration.

Entirely up to you, but does every single one of those blackboxes hide something worth hiding? Daevites makes sense, but as a reader there's no telling with the rest of it.

Footnotes
1. I mean, it's nothing to do with the David print of Napoleon directly above my computer desk or anything… certainly nothing to do with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I pride myself, quite rightly, on my ignorance of most of this site's headcanon. GOI's? No idea. 001 Proposals? Yeah I've heard the phrase, couldn't tell you what they are, really. I like to see each article as something in its own right, unique and breathing its own air in its own space.

This was a tricky upvote for that reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For those playing at home, there's a (probable) link to the Church of the Broken God here too. "Mekane" is six letters and so is the blackboxed name of the diety whose light of reason Napoleon bears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I read this not really understanding what was going on. The Daeva are long lost in my memory so I didn't make the connection until I saw it here; it's a hard read for us casuals. I still don't know who the god person is they are talking about. And without knowing the Daeva connection, it's very tedious.

Having said that, knowing it's Daeva completely turns it around and makes it an enthralling read, so I am casting a hesitant upvote.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded:
What happened after Napoleon crossed the Urals?

In my headcanon, he conquered the Daevites, executed the Daeva themselves and destroyed their history altering artifacts, triggering another ontoclysm which created this reality shard that bubbled up into the Foundation's history in 1822.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded:
1) What happened to the Daeva definitely fits in with the idea of reality quakes and I get the terminology from Kenneth Hite (who got the idea from Mary Gentle's Ash, A Secret History). The terminology works here and is better than some long-winded "the catastrophic event that separates Daeva history from ours" everytime.
2) SCP-140 isn't the only copy of the Chronicles. Somewhere there is a copy where the Daeva aren't destroyed by Ghengis Khan, but rather six centuries later by Napoleon Bonaparte. As those other copies get filled in, Daeva history gets closer to the present, and more and more reality shards get retrocreated, like deep rock pushed up along a fault line. This is one, the Antimatter Antipope is another, along with that temple in China, and the ruins where SCP-2140-1-D was recovered.

ETA: A tag search for "daevite, scp" shows that I missed a few. Of course that doesn't show articles like this one that aren't tagged (yet?).

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded:
1) I love that you're using the word ontoclysm at every opportunity.
2) I dig your explanation of how this still works with 140 with the idea that there is just a bubble of…Daevite probability around it, but for now, it doesn't really go past it. With that settled, I give it a +1, where I had been no-voting.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded: In my headcanon of this, it feels more of a painting from another reality that is designed to be understood by people from other realities/timelines. Which does bring the question: Why? What happened after Napoleon crossed the Urals?

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded: I think this is more like the Antimatter Antipope, than 140. This is evidence of the waveform of the Daeva ontoclysm being pushed closer and closer to the actual present (now they lasted until the 19th century). The waveform collapses around the artifact and that's why observers are from the history where this was true. That this bubble of the Daeva history exists, and that the Daeva are getting closer and closer have disturbing implications, which I really like about this!

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded: I would think that if the painting could retroactively modify history, the Foundation would have made a note of that, and classified it as a Keter. Since that's clearly not the case, I'm inclined to say that SCP-2515 can't modify history, but you might interpret it otherwise.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded: The Daeva one alters actual history to make the books true. Can this painting alter actual history, to the point that if the painting is observed by enough people, the Pass of Napoleon and the concept of Dea Europa could become part of it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, usually a D-class has a single number string, as if they survive testing they might then be assigned to a totally different SCP; however, this particular subject has apparently been reserved for testing with this SCP in particular, so adding the SCP number to his designation makes a certain amount of sense.

item.title: Re: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded:
If this were a Daeva artifact (and I understand that you may be going for a standalone scp), would it be possible that the original properties of SCP 140 would also be applicable here?

If it was a Daeva artifact, I don't see why the properties of SCP-140 wouldn't apply. However, it depends on your interpretation of whether SCP-2515 is a actual representation of historical events.

If this art historian were to continue to expound on it, would there be a possibility of the viewer developing new historical facts around details?

Not sure what you mean by "new historical facts", but if the art historian was, for example, to study Jacques-Louis David for a few years, he would probably have more to say about the painting. After all, the knowledge that viewers posses scales on their art history education.

item.title: Question about relation to other mentioned SCP
item.content:encoded: If this were a Daeva artifact (and I understand that you may be going for a standalone scp), would it be possible that the original properties of SCP 140 would also be applicable here? Art critiques are known for having leaps of conjectures. If this art historian were to continue to expound on it, would there be a possibility of the viewer developing new historical facts around details? Unless this was the obvious point in regards to retaining the historian, i'd love to hear if this was intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's very well written and it kept my attention, but the objects actual properties I found kind of dull. It's a painting which uploads information about itself to the viewer. So no vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd just like to say that "ontoclysm" is my new favourite word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I'm not really an artist, so my knowledge of perspective is rather limited. The way you describes it makes a lot more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm neutral on this for now. It's well written and quite enjoyable to read, but I'm not sure it adds up to much as a standalone piece of writing. As TL333s says, the only hint of story is through the description of the painting, and the anomaly itself is very vanilla. To get anything more out of this particular art course would require some quite specific pre-requisite reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really like this in general, but this line:

A star fort surrounded by corpses, with a pool of blood in its center, can be seen in the background of the painting, past the mountain pass being crossed.

set off my "the commissioner may be describing an escher-like perspective" senses. I think I have the perspective figured out now on my head, but the way that it's described here is a bit confusing. I think what you want is more like:

In the distant background of the painting a star fort is visible at the foot the mountain pass being crossed. The fort is surrounded by corpses, and the perspective is such that a pool of a fluid (typically identified by viewers as blood) can be seen within the fort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, and photographs of photographs of…

It's a recursive definition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something about this sentence really bugs me:

CP-2515-B is the designation given to all photographs of SCP-2515-A and all photographs of other instances of SCP-2515-B.

So -B refers to photographs of -A…and also to photographs of those photographs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree too. I upvoted it as-is, but that doesn't mean I wouldn't like to see this. It might fix a small niggling issue I have, which is that the explanation for Dea Europa doesn't seem all that odd to me, given the sort of ideas that were flying around in the Enlightenment. Not that they were all Gnostics, but certainly a deistic idea of divine Logic bringing light to the world isn't that incongruous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
scholarly disagreement is definitely a thing and could keep this from being quite so straightforward.

Frankly, I agree that it could be more interesting if multiple (and maybe contradictory) interpretations are seen through the painting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I get that, which IMO makes the level of exposition in the interview not only acceptable but required for versimiltude in this case.

That doesn't explain why this exists, or how this works, or where it comes from, or how this relates the Daevite ontoclysm, or if Dea Europa is the Broken God or anything. Which answers I personally would include in "almost everything".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the details in this, the star fortress especially. I like the use of a well-educated D-class to expound on the painting's history (for all that it's very expository, it's expository in the manner that someone would talk about something like this). But I feel like something's missing? I honestly wanted the accounts of those affected by this not to be quite so consistent. Say, obviously taking place in the same alternate universe, but with some questions as to what, exactly, is accepted about it. I mean, the only story here is the painting's existence and what it portrays; scholarly disagreement is definitely a thing and could keep this from being quite so straightforward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
individuals may claim to have knowledge about SCP-2515-A, despite SCP-2515-A not being found in any historical or cultural records accessible by the Foundation.

This is essentially saying that viewers gain knowledge (true or false or misinterpreted) of the SCP's context by looking at it (which is its anomalous effect), which lends itself towards exposition to explain the context behind the work. A saving grace is that the interviewed D-class happens to be an art historian and limits placed on the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does? How is this in our reality? Why does it impart knowledge of itself?

IMO, the only things that are explained are things that it would be reasonable to expect an art historian to know about the peice.
